ocr.text: In recent years, the significance of air quality monitoring has become increasingly apparent, particularly in urban environments
where pollution levels can fluctuate dramatically. This report presents data collected on April 1, 2025, from multiple cities
across North America, highlighting critical metrics such as PM2.5 concentrations measured in micrograms per cubic meter
(µg/m³) and associated air quality index values.
For instance, Philadelphia's air quality index recorded a value of 73, indicating a moderate level of air pollution that may pose
some health risks to sensitive groups. In contrast, the PM2.5 level in Houston was measured at 40.3 µg/m³, suggesting a
relatively cleaner air quality status compared to other cities. Additionally, Phoenix exhibited a PM2.5 concentration of 45.0
µg/m³, which underscores the ongoing challenges faced by urban areas in managing particulate matter. The data also reveal that
NewYork and Seattle present noteworthy air quality metrics, with Seattle's air quality index reaching 68. These figures reflect
the diverse environmental conditions experienced in different locales and the necessity for continuous monitoring and
assessment to inform public health initiatives. As cities grapple with the implications of air quality on community health and
environmental sustainability, such comprehensive datasets serve as a crucial resource for policymakers, researchers, and public
